data.title: Prologis forecasts $6.07-$6.23 core FFO per share while raising 2026 starts to $4.5B-$5.5B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Prologis (PLD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We entered 2026 with solid momentum, and we saw that continue in our first quarter results." (CEO & Director Dan Letter)
  • "We delivered another quarter of record leasing with 64 million square feet of signings supported by both strong retention and healthy new leasing activity." (CEO & Director Letter)
  • "Occupancy exceeded our expectations, and we are raising our full year outlook." (CEO & Director Letter)
  • "We are putting our land bank to work across logistics and data centers with $2.1 billion of starts in the quarter, of which $1.3 billion was data center build-to-suits." (CEO & Director Letter)
  • "We announced a $1.6 billion joint venture with GIC and subsequent to quarter end, a $1.2 billion joint venture with La Caisse." (CEO & Director Letter)
  • "First quarter Core FFO was $1.50 per share, including Net Promote Expense and $1.52 per share, excluding this Expense, each ahead of our expectations." (Chief Financial Officer Timothy Arndt)

Outlook

  • "We are increasing our forecast for average occupancy to a range of 95% to 95.75%." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • "This increase, together with our first quarter outperformance drives our expectations for net effective same-store growth to 4.75% to 5.5% and cash growth to 6.25% to 7%." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • "As for deployment, we are increasing development starts to $4.5 billion to $5.5 billion, this on an owned and managed basis with approximately 40% allocated to data center build-to-suits." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • "Putting it together, our strong start has us increasing our outlook on earnings." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • "Core FFO, including Net Promote Expense will range between $6.07 and $6.23 per share, while Core FFO, excluding Net Promote Expense will range between $6.12 and $6.28 per share an 80 basis point increase from our prior midpoint." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)

Financial Results

  • "We ended the quarter with occupancy of 95.3%, reflecting the seasonal drop we telegraphed and typically experience each first quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • "Retention remained very strong at nearly 76%." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • "Same-store NOI growth was 6.1% on a net effective basis and 8.8% on cash." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • "We sold or contributed approximately $1.2 billion of assets during the quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • "On our balance sheet, we raised $5.5 billion in new financing during the quarter at a weighted average rate of approximately 3.75%." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)

Q&A

  • Ronald Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: "Just any comments" on decelerating leasing spreads and the trade-off between occupancy vs. pricing; Chief Financial Officer Arndt: "there was some mix going on in the numbers" and "it's really not only market by market, it's really deal by deal," adding, "we are in a mode of pushing rents in a number of markets and situations, but still preserving for some occupancy."
  • Michael Griffin, Evercore ISI: whether data center pipelines face shelving/municipal pushback; CEO & Director Letter: "our pipeline in the build-to-suits for data centers is very strong" and "We've got 1.3 gigawatts of deals under LOI."
  • Nicholas Joseph, Citi: assumed margins on data center starts vs. prior commentary; CEO & Director Letter: "I would keep it within the range that you've heard us talk about the last few years. It's 25% to 50%."
  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo: why the occupancy guide leaves limited upside vs. Q1; Chief Financial Officer Arndt: "There is one, some pulling forward of occupancy" and "also reflects the strength of the pipeline."
  • Andrew Berger, BofA: latest 2026 fundamentals view; Managing Director Christopher Caton: "our view is unchanged" and "net absorption on pace to approach 200 million square feet and completions, 190 million square feet this year."
  • Vince Tibone, Green Street: durability/materiality of data-center-related logistics demand; Managing Director Caton: "It is a new structural driver of logistics real estate demand" and "less than 5% of new leasing a year ago to now 10% of new leasing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "Is that a risk for this pipeline?" and "it seems historically relative to historical trends" reflected a slightly negative/skeptical analyst tone centered on data center execution risk and transaction pricing. (Analyst questions)
  • "The risk today is that uncertainty slows customer decision-making" alongside "We have not seen meaningful evidence of that to date" reflected slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, while Q&A responses stayed measured, including "Every discussion, every deal is different." (CEO & Director Letter)
  • "In a few minutes, Tim will walk you through the details of our results" and "we enter 2026 from a position of strength" in Q4 2025 reflected a more broadly upbeat setup previously, while Q1 2026 added a more cautionary macro framing: "The conflict in the Middle East has introduced yet another source of economic uncertainty." (CEO & Director Letter)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "We delivered another quarter of record leasing with 64 million square feet of signings" followed Q4 2025 leasing of "57 million square feet of leases," with Q1 also adding, "March was a very active month for new leasing." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt; CEO & Director Letter)
  • Q1 2026 guidance language shifted to explicit increases: "We are increasing our forecast for average occupancy" and "we are increasing development starts," compared with Q4 2025 initial 2026 ranges including "forecasting average occupancy" and "forecasting development starts." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)
  • Data center momentum disclosures tightened from Q4 2025 "expanded our power access to 5.7 gigawatts" and "1.2 gigawatts currently in LOI" to Q1 2026 "Customer interest in our power sites is exceptional with 1.3 gigawatts under LOI" and "We ended the quarter with 5.6 gigawatts of energy either secured or in advanced stages." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)

Risks and Concerns

  • "The conflict in the Middle East has introduced yet another source of economic uncertainty" and "The risk today is that uncertainty slows customer decision-making." (CEO & Director Letter)
  • "Southern California is performing in line with our expectations" and "it is improving but will lag other markets," with "the 2 softest markets" described as "L.A. County and Seattle." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt; Managing Director Caton)
  • "Concessions are still a bit elevated right now" and "We do expect concessions to normalize as occupancies build." (Chief Financial Officer Arndt)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a quarter of "record leasing," raised full-year expectations with higher average-occupancy guidance and higher development-start guidance, and emphasized expanding Strategic Capital through new joint ventures. They framed the macro backdrop as more uncertain due to the Middle East conflict, but stated that customer plans are "unchanged" so far and pointed to continued demand signals in leasing, proposals, and data center build-to-suit activity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DraftKings plans to make Alberta its second Canadian province market
data.description:
DraftKings (DKNG) announced on Thursday that it plans to launch its online sports betting and casino products in Alberta, Canada, if it lands the required licensing and regulatory approvals. If granted approval, DraftKings Sportsbook and Casino intends to open on the province’s anticipated universal launch date of July 13/

Alberta would represent the second province in Canada where DraftKings (DKNG) operates mobile sports betting and casino products after Ontario. It would also mark the 34th jurisdiction in North America where DraftKings (DKNG) offers online sports betting and the seventh with online casino action. Of note, Alberta has a population of just over 5M, which makes it a larger market than the city of Los Angeles and close to the size of Colorado.

"We’re excited about the opportunity to expand DraftKings’ footprint in Canada and bring our online sportsbook and casino experiences to customers in Alberta," stated DraftKings (DKNG) VP Greg Karamitis. "With the anticipated launch aligning with the World Cup—hosted right here in North America—it's a particularly exciting moment for sports fans in the province to engage with our platform," he added.

data.title: London Stock Exchange seeing a ‘relief transaction trade’ amid Middle East tensions – CEO
data.description:
London Stock Exchange (LSEGY) (LSEG) CEO Julia Hoggett said markets are currently driven by a “relief transaction trade” as investors navigate geopolitical tensions surrounding Iran, with trading volumes remaining busy yet “orderly and rational” over the past several weeks.

The exchange chief noted that markets rally each time there appears to be progress toward resolution of regional conflicts.

In an interview with Bloomberg TV, Hoggett mentioned that the LSE has made significant progress on exchange-wide reforms, including executing its first trade in the private securities market.

Hoggett described the initiative as part of what she calls the “funding continuum,” a strategy designed to support companies at every stage of their growth rather than focusing solely on IPOs.

“If your sole relationship with a company is about an IPO, then you’ve got one product,” she explained.

Hoggett outlined a fundamental shift in how the exchange engages with companies, referencing Ronald Reagan’s famous skepticism about government intervention.

Rather than asking when companies plan to go public, the LSE now asks how it can help—whether that involves providing liquidity as a private company, raising funds, executing fund exits, or raising debt.

The CEO stressed the importance of eliminating the “cliff edge” between private and public markets, noting that earlier engagement with institutional investors in private markets can derisk the eventual path to an IPO.

“We have to recognize private and public markets live together,” Hoggett said. “It shouldn’t be that the only thing in common between the two of them is the company that goes through the process.”

Looking ahead, Hoggett set an ambitious five-year goal for the UK market.

“If in five years’ time that transition pathway is the smoothest anywhere in the world, we’ll have done our job,” she said, underscoring the exchange’s commitment to creating seamless execution certainty for all market participants.

United Kingdom tracking ETFs: (EWU), (FLGB), (EWUS), (FKU)

data.title: Semi equipment manufacturers dip despite Taiwan Semiconductor's high capex plans
data.description:
Semiconductor equipment manufacturing stocks were mostly down by Thursday afternoon trading despite Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) revealing during its first-quarter earnings it expects to hit the high end of its capital expenditures guidance.

TSM said its capital spending this year would be at ⁠the high end of earlier guidance, ranging from $52B to $56B. TSM Chief Financial Officer Jen-Chau Huang said the company spent $11.1B on capex during the first quarter.

"A very simple answer is, the demand is very robust, especially from the HPC and AI applications," said TSM CEO C.C. Wei when asked about the high capex plans during the earnings call. "And also, we try very hard to speed it up and pull in all the equipment as we can. Still, our supply is very tight. Demand is continuing to increase. And so we continue to work with our suppliers to speed it up. And that's why we are towards our high end of capex forecast."

ASML Holding (ASML) was down 4%. It provides TSM with extreme ultraviolet and deep ultraviolet lithography machines. On Wednesday, the Netherlands-based company increased its 2026 sales outlook.

Applied Materials (AMAT), which supplies TSM with deposition, etching, and cleaning machines, and metrology inspection tools, had inched down 1%.

Lam Research (LRCX) had dipped 1.5%. It creates tools for the etching and deposition processes. 

And KLA (KLAC), which provides process control and yield management systems used to ensure quality and efficiency in wafer production, had declined less than 1%.

data.title: Avis Budget and Hertz extend their dizzying 6-week rally
data.description:
Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) went back into rally mode on Thursday after a downturn on Wednesday. Both car rental stocks rose amid an elevated level of call buying.

Avis Budget (CAR) was up 10.6% in late afternoon trading and is now up more than 325% over the last six weeks. The stock hit a new 52-week high of $450.00 earlier in the session. Meanwhile, Hertz Global (HTZ) shot up 6.6% and is 74% higher over the last six weeks. Based on the last public information from trading exchanges, short interest on CAR and HTZ is over 20% of the total float.

On Wall Street, Deutsche Bank downgraded Avis Budget (CAR) to a Hold rating due to valuation concerns, while Hertz Global (HTZ) had already lost its last bull earlier in the year. Seeking Alpha analysts have been cautious on both names in recent articles.

Avis (CAR) and Hertz (HTZ) are both lined up to report first-quarter earnings in the next three weeks. Options volatility is already pricing in double-digit moves around the earnings releases.

data.title: Marsh expects 2026 underlying revenue growth similar to 2025 while targeting $400M Thrive savings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Marsh & McLennan Companies (MRSH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Overall, we grew revenue 8% in the quarter. Underlying revenue increased 4% despite lower fiduciary interest income and continued downward pricing pressure in insurance and reinsurance,” said CEO John Doyle, adding: “Adjusted operating income grew 8% from a year ago, and adjusted EPS also grew 8%.”
  • Doyle outlined executive changes tied to execution and growth, saying “Mark was named Chief Operating Officer of Marsh in addition to serving as our CFO,” and “We also announced Nick as the CEO of Marsh Risk,” while “Martin South… is now our Chief Client Officer” and “Ted succeeded Nick as CEO of Marsh Management Consulting.”
  • Doyle framed AI as central to strategy and margins, stating “Our strategy leverages our scale and capacity to invest in AI,” across “3 main pillars… growth… productivity… efficiency,” and said AI-driven automation and BCS initiatives are “building our confidence in continued margin improvement.”
  • CFO/COO Mark McGivney said, “Consolidated revenue increased 8% to $7.6 billion with underlying growth of 4%,” and added: “GAAP EPS was $2.36 and adjusted EPS was $3.29, up 8% over last year.”

Outlook

  • Doyle reiterated the full-year setup despite headwinds: “We continue to expect underlying revenue growth in 2026 to be similar to last year. We also anticipate continued margin expansion and solid adjusted EPS growth.”
  • McGivney provided key modeling items for the next quarter, saying, “Looking ahead to the second quarter, we expect fiduciary interest income will be approximately $80 million,” and “we anticipate corporate expense of approximately $90 million, which includes some one-off timing items.”
  • On the pace of margin expansion, McGivney said, “For modeling purposes, we expect to generate more margin expansion in the second half of this year than in the first half.”

Financial results

  • McGivney reported profitability and margin levels: “Operating income was $1.8 billion and adjusted operating income was $2.4 billion, up 8%. Our adjusted operating margin was unchanged at 31.8%.”
  • Segment performance included Risk & Insurance Services revenue of “$5.1 billion, up 6%… or 3% on an underlying basis,” with Marsh Risk revenue “$3.7 billion, up 8%… or 4% on an underlying basis,” and Guy Carpenter revenue “$1.2 billion, up 3% or 2% on an underlying basis.”
  • Consulting segment results included “first quarter revenue… $2.6 billion, up 11% or 5% on an underlying basis,” and Mercer revenue “$1.7 billion… up 11% or 5% on an underlying basis,” while Marsh Management Consulting revenue was “$897 million… up 10% or 6% on an underlying basis.”
  • Noteworthy items included litigation: “Noteworthy items this quarter also include a $425 million charge relating to litigation stemming from the collapse of Greensill Capital in 2021,” McGivney said, adding, “The charge in the quarter represents the best estimate of our liability in this case.”

Q&A

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: asked about margin expansion durability and “risks of AI disintermediation”; CEO John Doyle responded, “we've guided to year 19 of margin expansion this year, and we fully expect to do that,” and added, “we've competed with early-stage tech-enabled start-ups for a long time… and competed successfully.”
  • Peters, Raymond James: asked about buybacks vs. M&A given broker stock resets; Doyle said, “we want a balanced approach to capital management,” and highlighted “$750 million of buybacks in the first quarter,” while noting the pending acquisition: “We announced the acquisition of AltamarCAM… with about $20 billion of AUM.”
  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: asked about claims advocacy value-add and AI; President & CEO of Marsh Risk Nicholas Studer said, “Claims IQ… we've got several thousand colleagues now drawing on AI-enabled analysis of almost $200 billion of loss information,” and described upcoming launches: “the first applications powered by the Risk Cortex… are going to be launched in couple of weeks at RIMS.”
  • Brian Meredith, UBS: asked if AI productivity gains will be retained or competed away; Doyle said, “Our fees have been for a long time, stable as a percentage of premium,” and added, “if you think we're a discount insurance broker… but we're not.”
  • David Motemaden, Evercore ISI: asked for AI spend/partners and exposure to less-complex personal lines; Doyle said, “we've not shared that data in the past,” and added personal lines is “almost entirely a high net worth personalized client… It's not a material part of our business.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical and valuation-focused, pressing on AI disruption, margin sustainability, and capital allocation, including questions on “risks of AI disintermediation” (Peters, Raymond James) and whether AI gains will be “competed away” (Meredith, UBS).
  • Management tone was confident in prepared remarks and firm in Q&A, with Doyle using emphatic language such as “we fully expect to do that” on margin expansion and “we expect to be an AI winner,” while declining specificity on AI budget: “we've not shared that data in the past.”
  • Versus last quarter, the Q1 call leaned more heavily into AI execution detail (tools, automation examples, new “Risk Cortex” applications) and introduced leadership changes, while maintaining the same macro framing around pricing pressure and “headwinds from lower interest rates.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 introduced executive committee changes (McGivney adding COO; Studer leading Marsh Risk; South as Chief Client Officer; Moynihan leading Marsh Management Consulting) that were not described as such in Q4.
  • Guidance language remained consistent quarter to quarter, with Doyle repeating in both calls that 2026 underlying revenue growth is expected to be “similar to last year,” alongside “continued margin expansion.”
  • Market commentary stayed focused on softening pricing: Q1 cited “primary commercial insurance rates decreased 5% in Q1” following “a 4% decline in the fourth quarter of 2025,” and described reinsurance rate reductions (including “down 15% to 20%” for certain accounts) as a growth headwind but client benefit.
  • Q1 added a significant litigation-related item (the “$425 million charge” tied to Greensill), which was not referenced in Q4 prepared remarks.

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited “continued downward pricing pressure in insurance and reinsurance” and “headwinds from lower interest rates,” while pointing to mitigation via “strong sales,” Thrive/BCS efficiency, and AI-enabled productivity.
  • McGivney flagged ongoing legal uncertainty, stating on Greensill-related litigation, “this litigation is ongoing, so we aren't able to comment further at this time.”
  • Doyle cautioned that the “economic and geopolitical environment could change materially from our assumptions,” and discussed potential global economic uncertainty if Middle East conflict becomes “a sustained conflict in the region.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as “a solid start in 2026,” reiterating expectations for 2026 underlying revenue growth “similar to last year,” continued margin expansion, and “solid adjusted EPS growth,” while highlighting leadership changes intended to “accelerate pace” and a detailed AI roadmap tied to growth, productivity, and efficiency. The quarter also included a disclosed “$425 million charge” related to Greensill litigation, alongside ongoing capital deployment plans and continued emphasis on balancing acquisitions with share repurchases depending on the M&A pipeline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: The world is seeing a global inflation cycle upturn beyond heightened oil prices – Economic Cycle Research Institute
data.description:
The “window of vulnerability on growth is not open,” according to Lakshman Achuthan, co-founder and COO at the Economic Cycle Research Institute, who argues that a cyclical upturn in global inflation represents the defining economic shift of the current year.

In an interview with the Bloomberg Surveillance podcast, Achuthan emphasized that while many focus on headline-driven reactions, the underlying cyclical direction points clearly toward persistent inflationary pressures.

The inflation cycle has expanded well beyond U.S. borders, with future inflation gauges turning up “really cleanly” in Japan, Germany, and even China, where deflation fears had recently dominated.

Achuthan pointed to Japanese government bonds reaching a 29-year high and German bunds hitting a 15-year high as evidence of this global phenomenon.

“The global inflation cycle upturn, which preceded the war, is a thing,” he said, noting that rising commodity prices driven by the global industrial upturn began well before geopolitical conflicts emerged.

Achuthan expressed skepticism toward the market’s tendency for “knee-jerk reactions” and warned that the Federal Reserve may resurrect the word “transitory” if oil prices (CO1:COM), (CL1:COM) decline following any potential ceasefire.

“There’s a cyclical upturn. It goes much broader than simply oil prices,” he cautioned, suggesting that such labeling would miss the deeper structural forces at work.

Multiple factors are driving this persistent inflation, including bottlenecks in the system, supply chain disruptions, productivity that is “not exactly holding up,” and pent-up demand among higher-income consumers.

Achuthan noted that while there may be a “great longer-term story” around productivity gains from AI, those benefits are not materializing immediately enough to counter current inflationary pressures.

Market allocators hoping for an “inflationary boom” that could support equities may find themselves disappointed if inflation remains stubbornly elevated.

“That’s the beauty of cycles,” Achuthan explained regarding determining economic direction. “It’s on or off. And there’s a lot of noise. But what’s the underlying direction? Inflation is going to go higher.”

The Federal Reserve faces a challenging predicament as coincident data begins confirming the cyclical upturn, with core PCE exceeding three percent and core PPI approaching four percent.

Achuthan stressed that meaningful adjustments to risk outlook require examining both cyclical impulses and potential negative shocks rather than reacting to short-term headlines alone.

data.title: Via Transportation points to success with the Sioux Falls public transit project
data.description:
Via Transportation (VIA) updated in a LinkedIn post that the company has helped Sioux Falls restructure its public transit system so that nearly every resident can reach the whole city, including jobs, without additional budget increases. The city's Sioux Area Metro selected Via (VIA) as its technology and operations partner in late 2023, giving the company responsibility for day-to-day management, software, and route planning starting in 2024.

Via said it rebuilt Sioux Falls' network of 85 square miles around a combination of high-frequency fixed-route buses and citywide microtransit, extending coverage to 100% of the city and eliminating prior transit deserts in outlying neighborhoods. Notably, the system now uses data-driven routing to bundle riders into efficient shared trips while simplifying the bus lines so that core corridors run more often and with fewer long waits. Riders can book trips via an app that presents both on-demand microtransit and fixed-route options, making it easier to plan trips end-to-end.

The result is that Sioux Falls residents now have access to roughly 50K more jobs within a one-hour transit trip, nearly doubling overall job access compared with the old network, while lowering the cost per ride by about 25%.

Shares of Via Transportation (VIA) rose 1.4% in afternoon trading to $17.38. The transit tech company priced its IPO last September at $46 per share and traded as high as $56.31 before trending down.

data.title: MDU Resources started Neutral at J.P. Morgan as premium valuation offsets pipeline upside
data.description:
MDU Resources (MDU) trades little changed Thursday as J.P. Morgan initiated coverage with a Neutral rating and $22 price target, saying positive sales growth trends, regulatory diversification, and upside tied to favorable Bakken natural gas dynamics should help support growth durability, enhanced by successful execution of Bakken East.

However, JPM's Aidan Kelly said MDU's (MDU) current double-digit premium valuation largely reflects its growth runway, track record, and repositioning, leaving limited room for multiple expansion.

Under MDU's (MDU) base plan, Kelly sees a relatively narrow margin for execution against its targeted 6%-8% earnings per share compound annual growth rate, particularly when factoring in the spread between rate base growth and equity needs; that said, the analyst pointed to WBI Energy's pending Bakken East project standing out as a core lever that could lift realized EPS growth above the current range in 2029-30.

data.title: Stock market sentiment is reversing to pre-war tailwinds – Crossmark Global Investments
data.description:
The stock market’s rally to record highs reflects investors’ optimism that pre-war economic tailwinds will return as the US-Iran conflict appears to be winding down, according to Bob Doll, CEO, and CIO at Crossmark Global Investments.

“Prior to this war, the economy was pretty good. Earnings were better than pretty good. This war has been brief. And if it really is over or close to over, we’ll go back to those tailwinds, and that’s what the market is focusing on,” Doll said.

In an interview with Bloomberg Podcasts, Doll explained that the current market environment has created an unusual dynamic where nearly any news drives stocks higher.

“Why is this happening? Because good news is good news, bad news is good news, and no news is good news. You buy stocks,” he said.

The strategist also noted that the traditional investor base is shrinking relative to new market participants, including hedge funds, quantitative tools, and young individual investors who wield significant buying power.

Despite the rally to new all-time highs, Doll expressed caution about current valuations. He noted he would prefer a lower multiple than the current 21x earnings given the remaining uncertainty in the market.

While early earnings reports have been positive, he anticipates analysts’ estimates may need to come down as the season progresses, with many having hesitated to update their forecasts during the height of the conflict.

As geopolitical tensions ease, Doll expects themes like artificial intelligence (AIQ), (BOTZ) and private credit to return to the forefront of investor attention. He believes software stocks (IGV), (XSW) are “coming back from the dead” and that semiconductors (SMH), (SOXX), while undergoing a necessary correction, are not finished. However, he remains cautious about AI-related capital expenditure numbers, suggesting that expectations may need adjustment.

For investors seeking opportunities, Doll is currently favoring the financials sector (XLF), (VFH), (IYF).

“Look how much they’ve been pummeled since the first of the year. The numbers so far are pretty good. So, I would, on this weakness year to date, add to my financial position,” he explained. His investment strategy emphasizes price to free cash flow and high return on equity to ensure both quality and growth.

Looking at longer-term trends, Doll highlighted the importance of emerging market consumers for global economic growth, given shrinking populations in developed nations like China and Japan.

“If the planet’s going to grow, the emerging market consumer has to carry the day,” he said, describing it as a volatile but essential investment theme for the future.

U.S. markets tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).
